# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. movies that every cinephile should have seen/watched?

**Score:** 26
**Body:** im a new concious movie enjoyer and i was curious what are some movies that you think every person who wishes to get into movies/cinema should watch. like from timeless classics to niche movies like tampopo or etc. esstinally every movie one should have seen before calling himself a cinephile

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dr. Strangelove 
2. Vertigo, Rear Window, and Psycho. All Alfred Hitchcock. 
3. Sunset Boulevard 
4. Kurosawa .

Kubric.

Godard .

Tarkovsky .

Fassbender .

Herzog .

Tarantino .

Coppola .

Spielberg .

Scorsese .

Leone .

Bergman.



Most of the criterion collection , go by collections of directors is the best way. 

  - Criterion!! Not just the best movies, the foundational ones that all other films build on. 
5. You need to watch the great comedies to understand what makes human beings laugh.  It’s an amazing balancing act.  It needs to funny, and smart, but not too smart.  

Watch the Big Lebowski, but also Animal House.  Those are both hilarious comedies, but for different reasons.  

6. There will be blood. 

No Country for Old Men. 

Fargo. 
7. Cool Hand Luke 

JAWS

ROCKY

Godfather 

Godfather Part II 

Doctor Zhivago 

All Quiet on the Western Front  (1930)

Gone with the Wind 

Taxi Driver 

The Exorcist 

The Shinning 

The Misfits 

Schindler's List 

Tora! Tora! Tora!

Dracula 


8. Pulp Fiction 
9. Casablanca

The Maltese Falcon

Treasure of the Sierra Madre

The Big Sleep

Sabrina

The African Queen

## 2. What are some of your comfort watches or underrated films?

**Score:** 22
**Body:** Hey all, 

I’ve been sick the last few days and I’m trying to pass the time with some movies. I’m always on the prowl for more movies I haven’t seen and I keep finding myself doomscrolling trying to pick something new before I end up on one of my comfort watches like, “The Princess Bride”, “Superbad”, etc. My goal the next few days while I’m on the mend is to see at least 5 new movies, really any genre I’m game but preferably either comedy, fantasy, or adventure based movies. What are maybe some of your comfort watches when you are sick or some movies you really like that maybe aren’t as well known or mentioned as often. Thank you!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My Cousin Vinny is my "flu movie", The Lunchbox, Song of the Sea, Local Hero, The Dish (adorable Australian comedy that's super underrated), and Waking Ned Devine.
  - Waking Ned Devine is a superb movie! 
2. Dazed & Confused (1993)- a slice of life movie that takes place on the last day of school in 1976.   
The Fall (2006)- Lee Pace plays an injured stuntman in silent film era Hollywood, he tells fantastical stories to a little girl who is also in the hospital. Beautiful cinematography!  
Stardust (2007)- a fantasy movie where a young man adventures in search of a fallen star 
3. The Way Way Back

Always Be My Maybe

Role Models
  - I love the Way Way back also! ❤️ Pop n Lock
4. Baby Boom  
Tootsie  
Arthur
5. Mrs Harris Goes to Paris, Leap Year, About Time, While You Were Sleeping..
6. Communion with Christopher Walken, about ET encounters. A bit quirky. Oddly one of my comfort movies.
7. Freddy Got Fingered (2001)
8. Poor little rich girl 

A little princess 

Pollyanna 

The parent trap 


Matilda 


Cinderella 


Beauty and the beast

## 3. Looking for a silly/weird 80's comedy movies

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I am gen z and I was looking to broaden my horizons. I have recently seen innerspace which had martin short in it and I fell in love. I also watched a lot of Steve Martin movies and I love them. ( "All of me" being my favorite). I am looking for something that fits that cheesy vibe with a very interesting and weird plot. Magic, body swapping, sci-fi, curses, anything weird would do. 

I am not even looking for a masterpiece of a film just something that might be an entertaining night. 

Also something that might introduce me to new 80's actors... So I can brag to my mom :) 

Thanks!!!!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Big Trouble in Little China
2. Frank Oz’s Little Shop of Horrors; It’s a campy sci-fi masterpiece of a film with Rick Moranis, John Candy, and even Steve Martian himself. It’s incredible.
3. Better Off Dead (1985)

Labyrinth (1986)

The Princess Bride (1987)

Bill and Ted’s Excellent Adventure (1989)
  - All excellent choices! Better Off Dead is one of my favourites.
4. Three Amigos. With Martin Short 
5. Weird Science (1985)
6. Repo Man 
7. The Blues Brothers!
8. Time Bandits
9. Body swapping -- there was a period in the late 80s that this was THE thing.  18 Again!, Vice Versa, a few others (18 Again was the best).

Watch John Hughes' (director) entire catalog.

Ghostbusters

Stripes

Real Genius

Tootsie

The Naked Gun

Beverly Hills Cop

Airplaine!

  
That will get you started

## 4. I can’t recommend I Love Boosters enough

**Score:** 21
**Body:** A very unique and stylish comedy that I promise will have things here you will NOT expect. So funny and has a fantastic cast that all deliver. Also an anti capitalist message at its heart that hits hard. There is so much to say about this film but go see it in theaters and support so we can get more films like these

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sorry to bother you is equally brilliant. Do go and support boots!!
2. on it, thx
3. Literally sitting in a theater waiting for it to start. Very pumped. 
  - Have fun!!
4. It's on my agenda. He was recently interviewed on The Daily Show and he was awesome!
5. Sorry to Bother You was one of the most amazing experiences I’ve ever had at the movies.  I hope I get to see this in a theater too
  - If you enjoyed Sorry To Bother You then I think you will love this
    - I honestly didn’t enjoy I’m a Gemini.  I hope it’s more like STBY, or some brand-new thing that’s unlike either one.  Boots is one of those directors I’ll continue to support even if I don’t love a string of his movies, because I feel like he’ll always be doing something new.  
      - I've been a fan of Boots since I heard The Coup's Genocide and Juice in the mid 90s. I was really hyped when he started directing film and television. Can't wait to see this one, hopefully it comes to a theater near me.

## 5. I’ve seen very few documentaries. I’m looking for some very good, shocking, disturbing doc/docuseries. Just please nothing about abusive relationships

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I’m from the USA and have Netflix and Amazon prime and of course the free sites like tubi. 

I used to think I wasn’t into docs but I recently got into them and now I want to explore some more. What I like is very good and twisty, shocking and disturbing. Most of all I crave the shock. 

Abusive relationships isn’t something I can quite deal with though so please nothing with that. Or if there is some of that at least not the main focus. Other than that I’m okay with any other topic as long as it fits with what I said above. Thank you 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Woman Who Wasn’t There
2. "Please nothing about abusive relationships" in the title and then two of the first six comments are people recommending Dear Zachary, lol

Can anyone read?


3. Harlan County, USA

A classic documentary about coal miners strike. Won an Oscar for best documentary feature.
4. Watch all docs by Erroll Morris
5. The Bridge is pretty shocking as it shows actual jumpers. Also There's Something Wrong with Aunt Diane lives rent free in my head. Such a tragedy. 
6. Three Identical Strangers 
7. Wild country is very good. I don't remember any abuse in it beside the normal stuff that happens in a cult. 
8. American Nightmare on Netflix is very good with lots of twists and turns. 

A quick and satisfying fun one is Long Shot. Some incredible lawyering. 

You should obviously watch The Last Dance about Michael Jordan. I love sports docs, and there are a number of fantastic ones in the 30 for 30 series. OJ Made in America is another must watch. 
9. The Keepers (Netflix)
10. the jinx! one of the best ever imo

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What newer movies do you already consider modern classics?

**Score:** 1120
**Body:** For me, Poor Things is one that feels special. The cinematography is absolutely stunning and refreshing, Mark Ruffalo is hilariously unhinged, Emma Stone delivers an incredible performance, and the costumes/production design is out of here. I’ve seen it multiple times and it still impresses me every watch.

Another one is Hereditary. It felt like a fresh take on horror that I hadn’t really seen like it, and it has already influenced so many films that came after it with its particular brand of dread and family trauma. I haven had a horror movie shake me as hard as the last 20 minutes of Hereditary. 

What are some newer movies you think have already earned “classic” status? Why do they hit that level for you?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ex Machina became one of my all time favorite movies as soon as I watched it.

Arrival is another one that was phenomenally well done.
2. Blade Runner 2049
3. Her

28 Days Later 

Get Out 

Sentimental Value (personal favorite of the last year)

1917 

Arrival 

Parasite 

Lady Bird 

The Florida Project 
  - Parasite is really quite great. I think Her has staying power today as well.
  - 1917 doesn't get enough love. It's a masterpiece 
    - I normally don’t even gravitate towards war genre, but it’s really in a league of its own. The most captivating, heartbreaking story with monumental cinematography everyone needs to experience. I’ve never stopped thinking about it.
4. Flow

Maybe it’s because I’m a cat dad, but I was extremely impressed by how well it made you emotionally invested in a movie with no words, just an animated cat and other animals trying to survive in a flooded world 
5. I liked Bugonia too
  - I was gonna say Bugonia but I'm happy that I did not have to scroll far. Even though it is a remake of the original Korean movie, Bugonia was awesome.  I watched it on plane twice and I watched it again just a few days ago.
6. Nightcrawler

## 2. what is the single movie you sat through hoping it would get better, but didn’t?

**Score:** 662
**Body:** felt like a B movie. surprised it was acclaimed and won an oscar 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Wonder Woman 1984. It was like two different movies in one and they both sucked. 
  - "MAX LORD! YOU'RE PUTTING YOURSELF AND EVERYONE ELSE IN GRAVE DANGER! I NEED YOU TO GIVE ME THE STONE! WHAT HAPPENED TO IT?!"
    - I can hear her voice in my head as I read this. STOP IT.
      - Kal-el, no. 
2. Downsizing
  - Oh I liked it a lot, but it was NOT the movie that was advertised at all. If you went in the a quirky comedy looking at tiny living, you were in for a very different ride ..
3. Emilia Perez. I literally rage watched that film hoping to God that it would get better, but it did not.
4. The Brutalist
  - This was brutal to sit through. And so was Brody’s Best Actor speech.
    - which is still going

## 3. A must watch movie, Run Lola run is German made, and I 100% recommend it

**Score:** 457
**Body:** I just watched this masterpiece, and maaan I loved it so much, like everything about it is perfect, the cinematography, the edits, the music, a 10/10 movie

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Jason Bourne's first girlfriend
  - Funny story. She taught Matt Damon how to run on camera. They were doing a bunch of takes and he said he looked awkward until she showed him how she did it.
  - I haven’t seen this movie but it’s on my list now. I liked her in Bourne and hated that she was killed immediately in the 2nd movie. But it was essential to the plot and the audience was supposed to hate that. 
2. Fantastic movie!! 

![gif](giphy|ZgjCn4ZfpekWA)
  - They forgot their Lola repellent spray
3. Absolutely love this movie.

Fully recommended to anyone who wants to watch an action-based “Groundhog Day”.
4. I love the format of this move and how it’s all portrayed in real time.   Of course the electronic score is next level and really drives the insanely fast pace this movie manages to achieve with short planned breaks in the action just when you need them
5. i haven't seen this in so long. thanks for reminding me of this dope ass film
6. For half a second I thought that was LeeLoo and Korbin.
7. Saw it in film class. Opened the way for movies like drive. 

## 4. The legendary cinematographer Sir Roger Deakins turns 77 years old today. He worked on films such as Skyfall and No Country For Old Men.

**Score:** 452

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Happy Birthday, Sir Roger! IMHO he is my favorite cinematographer of the modern aesthetic. Through his eye, I feel there was such a deft balance between that modern minimalism while still retaining rich theatrical color portraits. Sometimes I feel the modern aesthetic goes a bit too bleak and washed out...where Sir Roger just seems to nail it.  
  
Blade Runner 2049 is a visual masterpiece. 
  - And the Academy had enough sense to give him an Oscar for it.
2. One of the greatest cinematographers of all time.
  - Yes! 
3. Happy birthday, sir Roger! Watched two of yours (Fargo, and No Country For Old Men) just in the past week. 
  - Fargo too?  This guy is awesome 
4. In college one of our lighting instructors was obsessed with him. Deakins is one of the greats, but the way this guy talked about him was almost biblical.

So my friends and I created a cinematography bible we called “The Gospel of Roger Deakins”. It ended up becoming very popular in our class, lol.
5. Holy shit, how did I not know this guy's name?

Big Lebowski? True Grit? No Country? Shawshank? Assassination of Jesse James? Doubt? 

This guy shot some of the most significant films of my lifetime.
6. Happy Birthday, Sir Roger Deakins! 🎂🎉🥳
7. Happy Birthday!  The scenes in Skyfall almost made me want to visit the locations in the winter

I said almost. 

## 5. What is your unpopular opinion of an actor?

**Score:** 378
**Body:** Might get hate for this, but I genuinely think Robert De Niro is a little over praised. Don’t get me wrong, he’s obviously a great actor and he’s been great in some all-time classics, but I feel like people sometimes act like every performance he gives is automatically untouchable just because it’s *him*. A lot of his roles fall into the same lane: the intimidating, emotionally guarded, street-smart tough guy with that slow-burn intensity. He plays that character incredibly well, but after a while it starts to feel more like variations of the same persona rather than complete transformation.

When I compare him to actors who disappear into radically different roles every single time, I don’t always see that same range from De Niro that people claim he has. He perfected a specific archetype and became legendary for it, which deserves respect, but I think the “greatest actor of all time” label gets thrown around a little too easily. Amazing actor? Absolutely. Untouchable once in a generation range? I’m not fully convinced.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think De Niro’s post 2000 filmography has probably soured his legacy a bit. But he has multiple performances that most actors can only dream of. I think he’s one of the greats. 
  - I was about to quibble with this and argue that Analyze This and Meet the Parents really showed his range and ability to get out from mobster and tough guys roles, then I looked it up and saw those movies came out in 1999 and 2000 respectively. I am old. 
2. You obviously have not watched Stardust 
  - https://i.redd.it/mt3n4jjyf63h1.gif
3. I hated Will Smith before the slap. 
4. I think Ben Affleck is a better director than an actor.
5. I think Keanu reeves is a wonderful human being and I love his movies and will see all of them at least twice. Now that all being said I really don’t think he’s a good actor.
  - I think he’s a great physical actor.  The John Wick franchise involves so much fight choreography and he does that really well.  Line reading not so much…
6. Lily Rose Depp is a bad actress. Idk why so many people praise her for Nosferatu. 

And her fillers are incompatible with historical films.
7. From the godfather to heat. Was a crazy twenty year run for robert de niro. I think he's very underrated in the movie awakenings. 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. How much does not speaking the language affect how we judge acting?

**Score:** 113
**Body:** I have a theory that acting often seems better when the film is in a language you don’t speak fluently.

I’m Norwegian, and I’ve noticed that when I watch Norwegian-language films, I am much more sensitive to whether a performance feels natural or “acted.” A line can be emotionally correct, but still sound slightly written, theatrical, over-delivered, or unlike how a real person would say it. Because Norwegian is my first language, I immediately notice the small things: rhythm, emphasis, pauses, slang, social register, awkward phrasing, and whether someone sounds like a person or like an actor delivering dialogue.

But when I watch films or shows in Spanish, Korean, French, Japanese, etc., I don’t have that same level of instinct. I can still judge facial expression, body language, timing, and general emotional truth, but I can’t reliably judge whether the line delivery sounds natural in that language. I don’t know whether a phrase is idiomatic, whether the intonation is off, whether someone sounds too polished, or whether the dialogue has that “nobody actually talks like this” quality.

I started thinking about this after telling a Spanish friend that a Spanish-language series was one of my favorites and that I thought the acting was amazing. He said many non-Spanish speakers had told him the same thing, but when he watched it, he thought the acting was not very good. That surprised me at first, but then I realized: of course he can hear things I can’t. He has lived a whole life inside that language. I haven’t.

So maybe foreign-language acting sometimes benefits from a kind of “performance halo.” If we don’t fully understand the language, we may be less able to detect stiff line readings, unnatural dialogue, regional awkwardness, bad accents, or subtle overacting. Subtitles give us the meaning, but they don’t give us native-level access to the texture of the performance.

This is not to say foreign-language acting is worse. Obviously not. Some of the best performances I’ve ever seen are in languages I don’t speak. But I wonder whether non-native viewers are often more generous because they are judging the visible emotional performance while missing some of the linguistic performance.

Do bilingual or multilingual people notice this? Do you find that acting in your native language is easier to criticize than acting in a language you only partly understand?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This is even true of accents. I'm Australian, and Australian actors using their own accent often sound to me like they're just... pretending. Even if I find them perfectly convincing in a put-on accent!

Outstanding actors (e.g. Cate Blanchett, Guy Pearce, Geoffrey Rush) can escape that feeling, but many very good ones don't.
2. I only speak English, and I'm pretty forgiving of "bad" acting in general if the story, themes, and ideas are strong enough. But I've had the same opinion as you for awhile. I really noticed it as foreign directors direct English language movies. The famous South Korean shifts in tone are MUCH easier for me to get on board with in a Korean movie, for example.
  - The tonal shifts in Parasite are just better done than in Okja, maybe it could be the acting but it's probably just the writing.
3. Interesting question.
I'm Swiss and one thing that I often notice with Swissgerman movies or plays is, when the people writing the dialogue either write in standard German or don't have the same dialect as the actors.
If actors have to memorize lines in sentence structures or with words that don't fit the vowel sounds of their dialect, it makes it seem stiff.

But since Swissgerman doesn't have "standard" grammar, it's very hard do write for every person, because most likely the actors will be from different regions.
Or they have to let the actors "translate" everything into their own dialect.
4. Completely agree with this - if we are not fluent in the performance language, we can't judge the acting well. That doesn't mean we can't enjoy or appreciate foreign language films, but I've always found it funny when people not fluent in a language comment specifically on the acting in a foreign language film. 

The other thing is, usually we need subtitles for such performances, which means that we can't pay direct attention to more subtle aspects of the acting (body language, smaller physical movements/gestures).

Silent cinema is of course an exception, and there are certain performances that are primarily physical (something like Mr Bean is almost universally popular for a reason).
  - But acting is more than delivery. So having an expressive face or body language is also important. 
    - Yes, but that's just a part of the performance - you can't assess the performance as a whole. 

And expressions and body language are also understood differently among different cultures. So, for example, it may appear that an actor is under- or overexpressive, when in fact according to their culture's codes that's not the case.

See also my comment about subtitles meaning we can't always focus on or notice body language and facial expressions.
5. I agree. And I think, for me, the fact that a lot of movies/series are dubbed in french exacerbate my perception. Let me explain: I am used to watch movies in their original language. My girlfriend and, more specifically, her daughter, watch almost everything in dubbed version. So my ears are deeply shocked when I discover the dubbing of sonething I know. It is pretty displeasant. But the way dubbing actors act is not so dissimilar of the way "normal" french-speaking actors do. I think it influences the way that I badly perceive acting in my native language. I think my perception  of canadian french is better because of the non familiarity with their accent.
6. You might have a point there. I have also sensed I judge the acting on films in my own language a tad harsher than I would do with films in English. I had semi-subconsciously thought acting chops were lacking here more often because perhaps our acting schools in general were not as developed. Hadn't thought that much about it, to be honest. But your idea makes sense to me.


The only times when this hasn't happened Is when I watch contemporary German films (German is not my mother tongue). I quite an average understanding of acting, but I often get the feeling (on many films, not all of them) that they "read" the lines, like everything feels very artificial, as if I were watching a soap opera, but I am not sure if there is another reason to it. There are exceptions, of course


Out of curiosity, what was the series you watched, spoken in Spanish?
7. Look no further than the way English speakers talk about anime - which is overacted in Japanese, on purpose, has its own style. But someone who only knows it from this media can't tell that and will complain instead that the English dub is overdoing it instead. 

Had the same experience but with English dubs before I learned English - that my native language one was cringe and bad, and English one could do no wrong.

It goes beyond acting too. It's about words and flow of dialog that always sounds more natural and less cringe when in foreign language versus your native one. That's why so many use random English words in countries that don't speak it.

## 2. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (May 24, 2026)

**Score:** 7
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **The Natural (1984, Barry Levinson)** — *The Natural* did not feel particularly cliched to me, which was a pleasant surprise, since I usually expect that to be my biggest complaint about inspirational sports dramas like this. On the other hand, I’m not exactly sure what it *did* feel like. I’m tempted to complain about how silly and mawkish it still feels, but it goes so far into it that I’m not entirely sure whether to evaluate it as a straight mawkish drama or as a subtle-but-deliberate parody of one. I guess I’ll just leave this as **Not sure how to rate**.

**Magnolia (1999, Paul Thomas Anderson)** — This was very different than I was expecting. Based on its reputation, I thought it’d be a classic hyperlink narrative, where a bunch of seemingly-unrelated storylines gradually converge into one. But that’s not really the case; while several connections between the characters do get revealed, the storylines still remain largely separate, with what convergence exists being more emotional and thematic than literal. It’s actually a remarkable achievement that Anderson manages to keep the entire thing feeling like a single, cohesive, and tightly-written narrative, given how little explicit connection there is between all its different threads. Pretty close to a perfect movie. **10/10**

**Munich (2005, Steven Spielberg)** — I'm not sure how to talk substantially about this movie without getting into things that are more radioactive than I’d like to talk about here. So, for now, all I’ll say is that this is a much darker and more complex work than I’d have expected from Spielberg, and he handles it beautifully. One of his best works, which is saying a lot. **10/10**

**Movie of the week:** *Magnolia*
2. Official Secrets (Netflix)

I Think You Should Leave (Netflix)

Remarkably Bright Creatures

—followed by—

My Octopus Teacher (both Netflix)

Out of the Past (TCM)

They Were Expendable (TCM)
3. A lot of Chanbara/Jidaigeki. I've come out of it feeling that Kurosawa is a perfectly competent director, but is a little overhyped in my opinion.

Favorites from this genre:

**Harakiri** - Tense, twisting, and with some incredible payoffs. The decay of samurai as a class is a common theme in these films, and I feel that Harakiri is where it reaches its zenith. (Questions of the historicity of the honorable or ideal samurai aside) At this stage in my life, I feel like movies that really hit this emotional register for me are few and far between. Go see this if you haven't already.

**Kagemusha** - An epic meditation on the illusion of power.

**Hitokiri/Tenchu** - A touching deconstruction of the samurai genre. Izo is a thug with delusions of grandeur; a loyal, fearless fighter who can't comprehend anything beyond the tip of his sword.
4. Catching up on 30 Rock again, watched The Long Walk, which I thought did a reasonably good job of adapting a dark novella; also watched Maxxxine which almost worked but failed to take its 80s set Giallo premise anywhere interesting or new.
5. Watched the directors cut of The Professional (Leon) , haver it on 4K and love the film but now its a little hard to watch certain scenes as knowing about Luc Besson but trying to separate art from artist. A great film with brilliant performances

## 3. Casual Discussion Thread (May 24, 2026)

**Score:** 3
**Body:** General Discussion threads threads are meant for more casual chat; a  place to break most of the frontpage rules. Feel free to ask for  recommendations, lists, homework help; plug your site or video essay;  discuss tv here, or any such thing.

**There is no 180-character minimum for top-level comments in this thread.**

**Follow us on:**

* [Letterboxd](http://letterboxd.com/truefilmreddit/)
* [Twitter](https://twitter.com/truefilmreddit)

The sidebar has a wealth of information, including the subreddit  rules, our killer wiki, all of our projects... If you're on a mobile  app, click the "(i)" button on our frontpage.

Sincerely,

David

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Streaming Arthouse and Indie films the week they release

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Hi all,

I'm researching whether there's demand for a streaming service that offers at-home theatrical releases (same week) of new indie and arthouse movies, i.e., sxsw/sundance selections, A24, NEON... For indie film fans living outside of big cities and don't live near arthouse theaters, who want to watch current releases. This would also serve as a community for independent film lovers to watch and discuss the latest films. 

Another mission of the platform is to serve as an additional revenue stream for indie filmmakers and studios that get a very limited theatrical release. Our platform will give them a much larger cut of sales than theaters. 

The platform would release films as they come out, just like a theater. A subscription gets you 4 credits to choose 4 new films to watch per month. 

If you live in a theater desert and feel like this is something you’d be interested in, please fill out this form! I’d also love to hear thoughts in the comments :)

[https://forms.gle/1EoBSa7JYTZJovga6](https://forms.gle/1EoBSa7JYTZJovga6)

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That sort of thing is a sure fire way to kill the few indie / art house / rep theaters that are still around. 

Most every indie distributor has streaming channels that, if not part of their company, at least cater to their particular niche(s).  MUBI, IFC, Shudder, Screambox, Criterion, and even Tubi!

I’m lucky enough to have an awesome indie microcinema near me with top notch curation, as well as a more mainstream “arthouse” theater that hits all the “critically acclaimed” Oscar-bait films. But I know if I miss something at one of them, they will inevitably show up on streaming *somewhere* soon after. 

Further collapsing of the theatrical window will just mean fewer and fewer theaters.
  - I get the hesitation and wanting to protect the existing independent cinemas, but I think people who want to go to the cinema will always go. For example, you would probably keep going to your cool local theater even if this product was available! 

This would indeed be another niche streamer, but serving as a catch-all for filmmakers without connections to the existing mainstream ones. 

Maybe to mitigate this issue a bit, we could make the release window further out in bigger cities, to protect the cinemas opening weeks revenue. 
    - And then you have a clusterfuck of a situation like you do with local sports, where the local game isn’t broadcast to its home market unless the stadium sells out , or other regional restrictions. 


I sympathize with wanting content to get more easily into the hands of those who aren’t in big population centers. 

Maybe take it up with ISP providers who’ve taken billions in tax $$ for rural access, and that hasn’t exactly happened, like, at all. 
      - That’s true, it could get messy… thank you for your input! 
2. I have significant demand for this streaming service not to exist. Also, terrible business idea, because you would have essentially zero moat against the established players. What should make indie film distributors want to distribute on such a niche service when Netflix and that 100 other streaming services already exist?
  - This would be more like a digital theater than a true streaming service. And the “niche” side of this is what makes it special! An indie service for indie fans :) 

The studios could very well hate this idea, but I’m just trying to gather information on whether there’s consumer interest here. So I’m curious, as a consumer, what don’t you like about this? 
    - As a consumer, I don’t want yet another streaming service to pay for that, by definition, has a very limited offering. Also, I want theaters to continue to exist.

As a filmmaker, I want my films to be seen by audiences as a shared experience in theaters. Having it go straight to streaming means fewer people will enjoy that shared experience. Then, when I go to streaming, I want to go where it will be seen by the most people (subscriber counts is arguably the best predictor of this with where your film will stand out as a secondary consideration).

Regardless, I don’t think this is a viable business model anyways.

## 5. Watched Salò, or the 120 Days of Sodom for the first time. With a "normie" friend. And we both were high as a kite.

**Score:** 0
**Body:** About a week ago, a friend of mine came across a video of Bob Odenkirk talking about Salò in the criterion closet, and really got curious about it. 

He told me about it, and it was pretty funny because he's not a movie nerd and he hasn't been exposed to many "experimental" or hard-to-watch movies like this one... so I thought it would be funny. I told him that I had heard about the movie before and about how completely fucked up it was, so we arranged to meet on Saturday to watch.

We smoked a big ass J and pressed play.

My life will never be the same again. And I'm not even dramatic.

It's a disgusting, brutal and heartbreaking masterpiece, but I never wanba watch that again.

I think it's a brilliant work that shows in the most realistic way what happens when you give the mentally ill complete control without consequences, and it is very relevant to our day, unfortunately. 

Also what do yall think about Pasolini's story... and did he come off as a hypocrite for criticizing such behavior when he was accused of having sex with a minor in the 1940s?

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. in fact this isnt pasolini's story , its based on a novel by De Sade. the movie itself is.. and i cant stress this enough, very tame compared to the novel . both really are timeless and a hyperbolic view into the morality of the ultra wealthy whom are both out of touch and out of reach of the average individual. many people cant look past the obscene examples used to portray the philosophical arguments. pretty sure pasolini changed the story slightly to mirror the current political climate of italy at the time of the movies release! quite a comical tid bit is that while theres only one scene depicting feces eating in the movie , its a common and central plot in the novel and is mentioned atleast every 3 pages.
  - Damn. I was actually planning on reading the novel instead, but I find feces especially disgusting so I guess it’d make more sense to watch the film and just skip over that one scene.
    - id read it for the writing style and backstory of the writer alone ! while the book itself beyond the philosophical monologues is pretty repetitive , id still consider it a worthwhile read especially if youre into either classical literature or philosophy .. or if youre into obscene gorey horror. De Sade was actually imprisoned at the time of writing 120 days and wrote the novel on smuggled in scrolls of parchment and it was thought lost for something like 200 years after the french revolution and the burning of the basille prison ! the novel itself is only around 1/3rd finished and the final 200 or so pages is simply just bulletpoints of plotlines he had planned to write.
